one_line_takeaway: A two-stage contrastive text-to-payload alignment method (SALM) modestly reduces shortcut learning in HTTP-payload threat classification under temporal distribution shift (68.1% vs. 65.7% best-baseline accuracy on real data, 24.4% vs. 20.6% on a synthetic OOD benchmark), but absolute accuracy stays far from production-grade and gains are concentrated in mid-frequency classes rather than the dominant or rarest ones.
key_finding: On the real, temporally-shifted (challenging) test split, SALM reaches 68.1% accuracy / 30.1% macro F1, versus TF-IDF+RF 65.7%/29.8%, FT CodeBERT+MLP 62.3%/26.0%, and Embedding Similarity 48.9%/20.9% -- reported as a +4% relative accuracy improvement and +1% relative macro-F1 improvement over the best baseline. On the synthetic out-of-distribution benchmark, SALM gets 24.4% accuracy / 14.3% macro F1 versus the best baseline (FT CodeBERT+MLP) at 20.6%/13.2% (+19% relative accuracy, +8% relative macro F1). Per-class F1 on the challenging split (Table II) shows SALM's largest wins on Dir-traversal (78.6 vs. best-baseline 63.0) and Info-Disclosure (70.8 vs. 63.0), while TF-IDF+RF remains competitive or better on the two largest classes, Code-execution (60.2 vs. SALM's 50.3) and Injection (69.8 vs. SALM's 69.2); several rare classes (DoS, RFI, Trojan) score 0 F1 for every method. The paper additionally notes the supervised CodeBERT+MLP baseline underperforms plain TF-IDF+RF under temporal shift, attributed to overfitting on imbalanced, shifted data, and shows via t-SNE (Figs. 3-4) that payload embeddings only become class-structured after Stage 2, since Stage 1 trains exclusively on text.
core_contribution: The paper introduces SALM (Semantically Aligned Language Models), a two-stage multi-modal contrastive learning framework for HTTP payload threat classification. Stage 1 fine-tunes a text encoder (initialized from hkunlp/instructor-base, a 110M-parameter T5-based instruction embedding model) with a triplet loss on vulnerability-description triplets (anchor/positive/negative sharing or differing vulnerability type) to build a vulnerability-type-structured embedding space. Stage 2 freezes this text encoder as a teacher and trains a payload encoder (also initialized from instructor-base) via a modal-alignment MSE loss (Eq. 3, Cached Multiple Negatives Ranking Loss in Stage 1, MSE in Stage 2) so payload embeddings are pulled toward the embeddings of their linked textual descriptions. At inference, classification is done by nearest-centroid semantic retrieval: each of the 15 vulnerability types is represented by a short generic textual prototype label (e.g., "SQL injection attack") encoded with the frozen text encoder, and a test payload is assigned to the class whose prototype has minimum cosine distance to the payload embedding -- deliberately using unseen generic labels rather than training-set descriptions to probe generalization instead of memorization.
threat_model: This is not an adversarial-attacker threat model paper; it studies ML generalization/robustness-to-distribution-shift for a defensive classification task. The setting is: an intrusion-prevention/threat-intelligence pipeline must classify captured HTTP request-response payloads into one of 15 vulnerability/attack categories (e.g., SQLi, XSS, Directory Traversal, Code-execution, Webshell, Trojan, Botnet, Scanner). The adversarial condition being tested is temporal/distributional shift: the model is trained on threats disclosed before a cutoff date and evaluated on payloads from vulnerabilities disclosed afterward (a 'zero-day' style generalization test), motivated by the observation that classifiers exploiting superficial lexical 'shortcuts' collapse under this shift even though they look strong under random splits.
limitations: The authors explicitly state: (1) evaluation is on a single cybersecurity task (threat/payload classification), so generality to other cybersecurity tasks (e.g., malware family classification, intrusion detection) is unproven; (2) 68% accuracy is 'far from production-grade reliability,' attributed to the vendor taxonomy mixing abstraction levels (techniques, outcomes, vectors), thin descriptions for some classes, and strong class imbalance (e.g., Code-execution has 167k payloads vs. Trojan's 72); (3) the temporal split tests generalization to novel techniques within already-seen categories, not true zero-shot transfer to entirely new vulnerability categories -- that capability is architecturally supported but not experimentally validated; (4) macro-F1 gains are small/near-flat versus TF-IDF+RF on the real split (30.1 vs 29.8), showing persistent failure on low-frequency and semantically overlapping classes (Trojan/Worm/Webshell); (5) the synthetic benchmark only covers 11 of 15 classes and its LLM-generated payloads may introduce artifacts not present in real traffic, limiting comparability across the two evaluation sets.

datasets_used: Private Huawei threat-intelligence corpus: 29,675 vulnerability descriptions, 601,518 HTTP payloads, 15 classes, temporal split at 2023-01-01 (train: 25,526 threats/517,692 payloads; test: 434 threats/83,826 payloads)

Synthetic benchmark: 11,000 balanced samples across 11 of 15 classes, generated by Gemini 2.5 Flash, DeepSeek-V3, and Qwen3-Max from public IoCs sourced from the PayloadsAllTheThings GitHub repository
quantitative_results: Real/challenging split accuracy: SALM 68.1% vs TF-IDF+RF 65.7%, FT CodeBERT+MLP 62.3%, Embedding Similarity 48.9% (SALM +4% relative over best baseline)
Real/challenging split macro F1: SALM 30.1% vs TF-IDF+RF 29.8%, FT CodeBERT+MLP 26.0%, Embedding Similarity 20.9% (SALM +1% relative)
Synthetic benchmark accuracy: SALM 24.4% vs FT CodeBERT+MLP 20.6% (best baseline), TF-IDF+RF 16.0%, Embedding Similarity 8.1% (SALM +19% relative)
Synthetic benchmark macro F1: SALM 14.3% vs FT CodeBERT+MLP 13.2%, TF-IDF+RF 9.0%, Embedding Similarity 4.3% (SALM +8% relative)

Fig.1 motivating example (easy random-split vs. challenging temporal-split accuracy, four prior methods plus SALM): TF-IDF 0.84 (easy) / 0.66 (challenging); Cross-Entropy Finetuning 0.94/0.62; Nearest-Neighbor Classifier 0.93/0.49; SALM 0.68 (challenging split value shown; easy-split bar for SALM not distinctly labeled in extracted text)

Per-class F1 (challenging split), Table II: Dir-traversal SALM 78.6 vs best baseline 63.0; Info-Disclosure SALM 70.8 vs 63.0; XSS SALM 85.7 vs TF-IDF 94.0 (TF-IDF wins); Code-execution SALM 50.3 vs TF-IDF 60.2 (TF-IDF wins); Injection SALM 69.2 vs FT-CodeBERT 73.4 (FT-CodeBERT wins); Webshell SALM 0.3 vs Embedding-Similarity 13.9; DoS/RFI/Trojan: 0 F1 for all four methods
baselines_compared: Fine-tuned CodeBERT + MLP on [CLS] token (supervised end-to-end fine-tuning baseline)
Embedding Similarity / RAG-style retrieval: Sentence-BERT (all-MiniLM-L6-v2) embeddings + HNSW index + k-NN majority voting
